symptoms.symptom_name: Multiple long-standing rough scaly keratotic lesions on chronically sun-exposed skin (field cancerisation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Actinic keratosis with acute traumatic inflammation and minor haemorrhage
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A field of multiple long-standing photodamaged lesions makes a traumatised actinic keratosis the most likely source of the symptomatic spot.
Field cancerisation is itself a risk factor for SCC - the same UV damage that causes AKs drives invasive keratinocyte cancers.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute change in a single chronic lesion - new pain, inflammation and bleeding over 24 hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A chronic actinic keratosis that acutely changes, becomes tender and bleeds mandates active exclusion of invasive SCC regardless of the lower prior probability.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Actinic keratosis with acute traumatic inflammation and minor haemorrhage
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Minor trauma to a friable hyperkeratotic lesion readily produces transient inflammation and a haemorrhagic crust.

symptoms.symptom_name: Anticoagulation with apixaban for atrial fibrillation

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Actinic keratosis with acute traumatic inflammation and minor haemorrhage
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Anticoagulation explains why a minor traumatic scrape to a sun-damaged lesion bled readily, without implying a more sinister cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Minor, self-limiting bleeding that settled with light pressure

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Actinic keratosis with acute traumatic inflammation and minor haemorrhage
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Minor bleeding that stops with pressure is consistent with superficial trauma rather than a deeply invasive or vascular tumour.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever, no spreading erythema, no purulence, systemically well

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever, spreading erythema and pus with a systemically well patient allows clinical (history-level) exclusion of secondary cellulitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Flat scaly lesion without a rapidly enlarging hard nodule or deep non-healing ulcer (patient-described morphology)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A flat scaly patch without a firm growing nodule or persistent ulcer lowers, but by no means excludes, invasive SCC - the patient cannot reliably assess induration.

symptoms.symptom_name: Lesion base induration / firm indurated nodule on palpation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Induration of the base is a key discriminator for invasive SCC - absent on the in-person source exam, but this cannot be palpated remotely, which is why in-person review is required.
Regional nodes would raise concern for metastatic SCC - absent on in-person exam, not assessable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dermoscopy / histological (biopsy) characterisation of the symptomatic lesion

investigation_details.investigation_name: Skin biopsy of symptomatic lesion (with dermoscopy)
investigation_details.expected_result: Dermoscopy and, if indicated, biopsy would characterise the lesion and distinguish actinic keratosis / in-situ change from invasive squamous cell carcinoma - the only definitive discriminator, not achievable via telehealth.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: A potential SCC on a field-cancerised scalp is not histologically excluded and may progress undiagnosed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Histology is the definitive means of excluding or confirming invasive SCC in a changed keratinocytic lesion.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A single chronic lesion that acutely changes on a background of extensive field cancerisation is the specific pattern that mandates in-person examination and biopsy to exclude invasive SCC - neither feature alone is as concerning as their combination.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person dermoscopy of the symptomatic lesion
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Dermoscopy by a GP or skin-cancer clinician is the appropriate next step to characterise the changed lesion; it cannot be performed via telehealth.
Biopsy is required if the in-person assessment cannot confidently exclude SCC - the definitive investigation, arranged in person.
No purulence, spreading erythema, fever or systemic upset - there is no clinical infection to investigate or treat; a swab and empiric antibiotics would be over-investigation and over-treatment.
